subject: Science
big_idea: The biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere are interconnected, as matter cycles and energy flows through them.
learning_goals: I can describe the four main Earth spheres.
I can identify when more than one sphere is involved in an event or change.
I can explain how a change in one sphere can affect another.

content.subtitle: How are Earth’s spheres connected, and why does that matter when something changes?

content.scenario: Heavy rain hits a steep hillside. Soil washes into a river. The water gets muddy. Fish habitat changes.
prompts: What parts of Earth seem connected?

rows: A system is made of parts that affect each other.
Scientists use spheres as a model to organize connected parts of Earth.
The spheres help explain how changes move through Earth systems.

left.title: Atmosphere + hydrosphere
items: Atmosphere: air, gases, weather, smoke, clouds
Hydrosphere: rivers, lakes, rain, snow, ice, groundwater

right.title: Geosphere + biosphere
items: Geosphere: rocks, soil, land, sediment, ground
Biosphere: living things such as plants, animals, and people

content.header: Which sphere does each example fit best?

left: Heavy rain hits a hillside and loosens soil.
The geosphere changes as the soil becomes easier to move.
right: Loose soil moves into a river and changes the hydrosphere.
Fish habitat changes in the biosphere because the water conditions change.

content.scenario: Wildfire smoke fills the air. Plants are damaged. Rain later washes ash into streams.

content.task: Identify the spheres involved and explain at least one interaction chain.

rows: The four spheres help organize Earth systems.
Strong explanations trace how one change affects another.
Earth events are not isolated facts. They are system interactions.

goals: I can describe the four main Earth spheres.
I can identify when more than one sphere is involved in an event or change.
I can explain how a change in one sphere can affect another.

supported.tip: Ask: What changed first? What changed next?

questions.q_text: A hillside gets heavy rain. Soil washes into a river. Name two spheres involved and give one example for each.
Sort these examples into the sphere they fit best: rainfall, fish, soil, smoke, groundwater, tree roots.
Complete this idea: Heavy rain affects the ______ sphere first. Then it affects the ______ sphere because ______.
Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Explain one way a change in one sphere could affect another.

self_check: Did you name the four main spheres accurately?
Did you show at least one connection instead of only listing spheres?

anchor: A strong explanation does more than label the spheres involved in an event.
It traces how one change affects another and uses scientific reasoning to show the connection.

questions.q_text: Define each of the four main Earth spheres and give one example for each.
Sort these examples into the sphere they fit best and justify one tricky choice: rainfall, fish, rock, clouds, tree roots, glacier melt, soil, algae, smoke, groundwater.
Explain how heavy rain on a hillside can affect more than one sphere. Use at least two because or affects statements.
Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Identify the spheres involved and explain at least one interaction chain clearly.

self_check: Did you justify at least one sorting choice?
Did you explain a real cause-and-effect chain?
answer_key: algae, fish, and tree roots → biosphere

anchor: The sphere model is useful because it helps scientists explain systems, not just describe separate parts.
Real events often involve overlapping causes and multiple effects.

questions.q_text: Explain why the sphere model is more useful than describing a natural event as one isolated fact. Use an example.
Sort the examples and explain one case that could seem to fit more than one sphere: rainfall, fish, rock, clouds, tree roots, glacier melt, soil, algae, smoke, groundwater.
Analyze the wildfire smoke to plant damage to ash in streams example. Explain the interaction chain with precise scientific language.
Using the Big Idea wording, argue why understanding Earth systems as interconnected leads to stronger scientific explanations than simply naming land, air, water, and living things.

self_check: Did you handle an ambiguous case with a reasoned explanation?
Did you use the Big Idea wording in a meaningful way?
answer_key: Stronger answers explain overlap without losing the best-fit classification.
Atmosphere to biosphere to hydrosphere is a common wildfire chain.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Event analysis
rubrics.question: Choose one event: wildfire, flood, drought, or melting snowpack. Identify the spheres involved and explain at least one interaction chain clearly.

criteria.emerging: Names some relevant parts of the event but gives little explanation of how they connect.
criteria.developing: Identifies the spheres involved and gives a partial explanation of one interaction.
criteria.proficient: Clearly explains at least one interaction chain using accurate sphere language and cause-and-effect reasoning.
criteria.extending: Explains the interaction chain clearly and precisely, showing why the system model strengthens the explanation.

target_understanding: Students understand that the four spheres are a model for organizing Earth systems.
Students can identify when more than one sphere is involved in one event.
Students can explain at least one interaction chain instead of only naming categories.

watch_for.misconception: Students think the spheres are separate boxes with no overlap.
watch_for.redirect: What changed first, and what did that change affect next?

watch_for.misconception: Students list spheres without explaining how they interact.
watch_for.redirect: Can you show the connection with affects, leads to, or because?

timing.activity: Introduce system idea and the four spheres
Model and discuss one interaction chain
Worksheet event analysis and guided support

teacher_notes: Keep the opening concrete. Do not start with a vocabulary dump.
The key move is from naming categories to explaining interactions.
Use local or familiar events where possible to make transfer easier.
